steps.action: Singkil is a Maranao royal dance from the Philip with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: Singkil is a Maranao royal dance from the Philip with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: The dance features a prince and a princess weavi
steps.text: The dance features a prince and a princess weaving in and out of crisscrossed bamboo poles clapped in syncopated rhythm.

steps.action: The dance takes its name from the belled accesso
steps.text: The dance takes its name from the belled accessory worn on the ankles of the Maranao princess.

steps.action: A kulintang and agung ensemble always accompanie
steps.text: A kulintang and agung ensemble always accompanies the dance.

steps.action: Sani (1979) suggests that the dance originated i
steps.text: Sani (1979) suggests that the dance originated in the Basak area, located on the eastern shores of Lake Lanao, which then spread to other villages in the 1930s.

steps.action: On the other hand, de los Santos (1979) claims t
steps.text: On the other hand, de los Santos (1979) claims that the dance was brought to Lanao, specifically to Rumayas, Lumba-Bayabao (formerly known as Maguing), by an individual from Cotabato, Maguindanao.

steps.action: Although de los Santos does not provide a specif
steps.text: Although de los Santos does not provide a specific date for its introduction, it is believed that the Singkil dance appeared relatively recently.

steps.action: Abdullah Madale (1976) includes a photograph in
steps.text: Abdullah Madale (1976) includes a photograph in his book, labeling it as the Singkil dance and depicting two parallel bamboo poles.

steps.action: He describes it as a typical outdoor dance of th
steps.text: He describes it as a typical outdoor dance of the Maranao people.

steps.action: Similarly, Steven Fernandez, the founder and art
steps.text: Similarly, Steven Fernandez, the founder and artistic director of the Integrated Performing Arts Guild (IPAG), speculates that the Singkil dance may have originally been a dance-game played by Maranao children.

steps.action: However, it underwent a significant transformati
steps.text: However, it underwent a significant transformation into a noble royal dance named Singkil by the Bayanihan Philippine National Folk Dance Company.

steps.action: Henrietta Hofer-Ele was one of the first to cond
steps.text: Henrietta Hofer-Ele was one of the first to conduct a research on the execution of the dance which is now called Singkil.

steps.action: Encouraged by her teacher, Francisca Reyes Aquin
steps.text: Encouraged by her teacher, Francisca Reyes Aquino, Hofer-Ele conducted her study on a unique bamboo dance that she had witnessed in Marawi during a regional inter-school athletic event.

steps.action: Initially, the dance had no name and was perform
steps.text: Initially, the dance had no name and was performed only by girls, usually one or two dancers, holding fans in their hands.

steps.action: Aquino had intended to study the dance herself b
steps.text: Aquino had intended to study the dance herself but lacked local connections, which hindered her investigation.

steps.action: In the mid-1950s, Hofer-Ele encountered difficul
steps.text: In the mid-1950s, Hofer-Ele encountered difficulties finding individuals knowledgeable about the dance since it was rarely practiced and gradually fading away.

steps.action: While Hofer-Ele's research did not provide evide
steps.text: While Hofer-Ele's research did not provide evidence that the dance was originally a royal dance, the Bayanihan Philippine National Folk Dance Company, to which she later presented her findings, interpreted it as such.

steps.action: This interpretation may have been influenced by
steps.text: This interpretation may have been influenced by the fact that the person who taught Hofer-Ele the dance was of royal descent.

steps.action: Bayanihan made adjustments to the dance, includi
steps.text: Bayanihan made adjustments to the dance, including designating the principal dancer as a "princess" accompanied by an attendant holding a royal umbrella.

steps.action: The dance was subsequently named Singkil after t
steps.text: The dance was subsequently named Singkil after the brass anklets worn by the original dancer, Princess Tarhata.

steps.action: Later, the Bayanihan folk dance group incorporat
steps.text: Later, the Bayanihan folk dance group incorporated the Singkil dance into a storyline based on an episode from the Maranao epic, Darangen.

steps.action: Bayanihan played a significant role in populariz
steps.text: Bayanihan played a significant role in popularizing Singkil and introducing notable modifications to the original dance.

steps.action: Creative directors of the group reimagined the d
steps.text: Creative directors of the group reimagined the dance in the early 1950s to enhance the cast and characters for their world tour at the Brussels Expo in 1958.

steps.action: Their version of Singkil features multiple fan d
steps.text: Their version of Singkil features multiple fan dancers, a prince, warriors with swords and shields, crisscrossed bamboo poles, and an umbrella attendant.
